title: Northveil's West Texas Wind Plans Hang in Balance as Bond Deal Unravels
body:
The collapse of Northveil Energy Partners' $500 million green bond offering has thrown the company's planned 800-megawatt wind expansion in Culberson County into uncertainty, project documents reviewed by this publication show.

Northveil had already signed turbine supply agreements with a European manufacturer and begun permitting work on the West Texas project, which was expected to come online in phases through 2028. Without the bond proceeds, the company faces a financing gap it has not publicly explained how it will close.

Warren Gault, Northveil's chief financial officer, has said the company is pursuing alternative financing and that the project timeline has not changed. But two people familiar with the company's capital planning said privately that a delay of at least six months is now likely.

The wind farm, if completed, would be one of the larger renewable additions to the ERCOT grid in recent years. State energy regulators had flagged the project as a priority given growing summer peak demand.

Industry observers have also noted that the ESG downgrade that torpedoed the bond came at a particularly bad time: Northveil was trying to shed its historical association with natural gas gathering, a business it has been winding down. The Sustain Ratings action effectively froze that rebranding in mid-pivot.

The company declined to make executives available for this article beyond a brief emailed statement from Gault.
